patches.find: El Gordo is a genuine 6.2σ ΛCDM anomaly, but citing an opponent's challenge without providing an alternative prediction is non-discriminating cherry-picking; the dome has no cosmological model, no structure formation equations, and no spatial framework for galaxy clusters
patches.replace: El Gordo is a genuine 6.2σ ΛCDM anomaly (Asencio et al. 2023) that remains unresolved as of 2025. The dome correctly identifies this tension but provides no alternative: 'aetheric condensation' has no equations, no cluster mass function, and no velocity predictions. The dome's 20,015 km disc has no spatial framework for objects at z = 0.87. Citing a competitor's unsolved problem without offering your own solution is non-discriminating.
patches.rationale: EXP-022 integration: Improved finding with explicit citation, more specific description of what the dome lacks, and cleaner statement of the non-discriminating critique.

patches.find: El Gordo (ACT-CL J0102-4915) is a massive merging cluster at z ≈ 0.87 with total mass ~2.1 × 1015 M☉. Asencio, Banik & Kroupa (2023, ApJ 954, 162) found El Gordo is in 6.2σ tension with ΛCDM using nominal mass and velocity values — a genuine challenge. However, the dome model exploits this tension without providing any alternative: it has no structure formation equations, no dark matter dynamics, no nucleosynthesis predictions, and no framework for galaxy clusters at all. The dome's physical universe is capped at disc_radius = 20,015 km and firmament_height = 9,086 km — there is no spatial framework in which galaxy clusters at z = 0.87 can exist. Identifying a problem with ΛCDM is not the same as solving it.
patches.replace:
El Gordo (ACT-CL J0102−4915) is one of the most massive galaxy cluster mergers known: total mass M200c ≈ 2.13+0.25−0.23 × 1015 M☉ at redshift z = 0.87 (Kim et al. 2021, ApJ 923, 101). Hydrodynamical simulations by Zhang et al. (2015) found that reproducing El Gordo's X-ray morphology, luminosity, and temperature requires an infall velocity Vinfall ≥ 2,500 km s−1 — a collision speed that is extremely improbable under ΛCDM hierarchical assembly at z = 0.87. Asencio, Banik & Kroupa (2023, ApJ 954, 162) quantified this as a 6.2σ tension using updated mass and velocity estimates. Even with the revised lower mass from Kim et al. (2021), reducing the tension below 5σ requires Vinfall < 2,300 km s−1 — but no simulation has reproduced El Gordo's observed properties at such low velocities. A 2024 study (Molnar & Birkinshaw 2024, A&A) confirmed El Gordo remains "extremely unlikely within standard ΛCDM" and explored self-interacting dark matter (SIDM) as a potential resolution, though required cross-sections exceed current observational limits.

This is a genuine, unresolved challenge to ΛCDM. The dome model correctly identifies it. However, the dome's proposed accommodation — "no expansion epoch; aetheric condensation on thermal timescale; high-velocity collisions natural" — is a bare assertion, not a physical model. The dome provides: (1) no equations for structure formation or cluster assembly, (2) no cluster mass function predicting how many clusters of mass M exist at redshift z, (3) no mechanism by which 'aetheric condensation' forms 1015 M☉ structures, (4) no calculation of expected collision velocities, and (5) zero monitoring infrastructure for cosmological claims. The dome's physical universe is capped at disc_radius = 20,015 km with firmament_height = 9,086 km — there is no spatial framework in which galaxy clusters at cosmological distances can exist. Stating "no expansion epoch" removes one constraint but provides no replacement physics: without equations, the dome cannot predict whether its model produces El Gordo or not. That is not accommodation — it is silence.
patches.rationale: EXP-022 integration: Full replacement with proper citations (Kim et al. 2021, Zhang et al. 2015, Molnar & Birkinshaw 2024), exact mass with uncertainties, clearer explanation of why lower mass doesn't resolve the tension, and specific enumeration of what the dome lacks.

patches.find: El Gordo does genuinely challenge ΛCDM (6.2σ tension per Asencio et al. 2023). However, citing an opponent's anomaly as your own WIN is non-discriminating: even if ΛCDM completely failed for El Gordo, this would not constitute evidence for the dome model. The dome has no cosmological structure formation framework, no spatial model for objects beyond disc_radius = 20,015 km, and no prediction for galaxy cluster collision velocities. 'Not Demonstrated' (non-discriminating) is the correct verdict: the evidence cannot distinguish between dome and globe models because the dome makes no prediction about galaxy clusters whatsoever.
patches.replace: El Gordo genuinely challenges ΛCDM at 6.2σ significance (Asencio et al. 2023), and we do not dispute this. The dome model deserves credit for citing a real, peer-reviewed tension rather than a fabricated problem. However, identifying a competitor's difficulty is not the same as demonstrating your own model's superiority. The dome offers 'aetheric condensation on thermal timescale' as its accommodation but provides no equations, no mass function, no velocity predictions, and no spatial framework for extragalactic objects. 'Not Demonstrated' is the correct verdict: the dome has not demonstrated that its model can form galaxy clusters, predict their properties, or produce an outcome different from ΛCDM. Removing the expansion epoch is a necessary but not sufficient condition — you must also show what replaces it.
patches.rationale: EXP-022 integration: Improved detail_verdict_text that acknowledges the genuine ΛCDM challenge before critiquing, gives credit to the dome for citing real peer-reviewed tension, then delivers the non-discriminating critique.
